summary: The width-reservation fix is correct: naReserve right-aligns the n/a sentinel into the exact column slot the value occupies (cache hit ratio %6.2f%% = width 7; workload rates via naInt at widths 4/3), verified against pretty.ReserveWidth which uses %*d. The new offset-equality test directly proves the trailing label stays static, build and tests pass, and the 'deliberately not changed' reasoning (bgwr/ckpt composite, pretty.Size variable-width fields) is sound. No critical or major issues found.

suggestions.suggestion: Test_renderPgstat_verboseNAWidthStatic asserts the static-label invariant explicitly (strings.Index equality) only for the 'cache hit ratio' field. The workload-rate path (naInt -> naReserve at width 4/3) relies solely on golden-string equality spread across two separate tests (verboseNA vs verboseAvailable), so its offset-staticness is only implied, not directly asserted. Consider extending the offset-equality assertion to one workload field (e.g. 'tps') to lock in the naInt path against the same regression class.
suggestions.benefit: Directly guards the naInt/naReserve(width) path with the same byte-offset invariant used for cache hit ratio, instead of relying on a human noticing that two golden strings happen to align.

suggestions.suggestion: naReserve's min-width guard (width < len(naLiteral) -> clamp to 3) keeps n/a from being truncated, but the static-alignment invariant only holds when the value side also reserves >= the same effective width. This is correctly stated in the doc comment ('the value side must reserve the same effective width'). All current call sites use width >= 3, so no action is needed now; just noting the invariant is enforced by convention, not by code, should a future caller pass width < 3.
suggestions.benefit: Documents the implicit contract so a future narrow-width caller does not silently break label alignment.
